title: Quick check, inbound routing and IVR

questions.prompt: A customer reports that calls to their main number land in the after-hours IVR during the day. The CDR Timeline View shows the time condition fired at 11am local time. What's the first thing to check?
choices: Whether the PBX's system timezone and the per-route Time Zone field agree with the customer's actual location
Whether the IVR's Prompt Repeat Count is set too low
questions.explanation: The PBX's system timezone (PBX Settings → Date and Time) and the per-route Time Zone field are separate settings. If they disagree, business-hours rules fire at the wrong wall-clock times. Always check both before changing anything else.

questions.prompt: An IVR with the Dial Outbound Routes option enabled lets callers make outbound calls from the IVR. This is safe to enable on a public-facing IVR because PSE has built-in protections.
questions.explanation: Allowing outbound calls from an IVR is a classic toll-fraud vector. A caller dials in, navigates to outbound, dials an international number, and the customer foots the bill. Leave Dial Outbound Routes disabled on customer-facing IVRs unless the customer has a deliberate calling-card pattern with PIN restrictions.

questions.prompt: Put these inbound routing destination decisions in the right order from simplest to most complex, given a customer with one main number.
items: All Time mode, single destination, rings reception extension directly
Time-condition with business hours and holiday handling, three destinations
Business-hours IVR with several key-press destinations including a queue, plus after-hours and holiday paths
Call Flow Designer flowchart with conditional logic and a database lookup before routing
questions.explanation: Each tier adds capability. Start at the simplest that solves the customer's stated need; only climb if their requirements force it. Most small businesses are happy at tier 1 or 2.
